## PAGE 00423: Dependency Matrix

- page_id: `136709844`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709844/Dependency+Matrix
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T14:48:30.405+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Analysis diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308903551

308903553

308903552

Demo introducing Dependency Matix.

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions only.

The Dependency Matrix is a quick method for representing dependencies compactly. Diagrams, UML elements, and extended UML elements serve as row and column entries. The cells in the matrix show where these elements are associated (related).

Dependency matrices can show dependency criteria, such as

- UML relationships
- BPML, SysML, UPDM, and other DSL relationships
- Semantic dependencies (dependencies through properties)
- Relationships through tags (tags allow for relating UML elements that cannot be represented on the same diagram)
- Transitive relationships (through the OCL or meta chain)

Dependency matrices are useful for

- Quickly visualizing and creating dependency criteria.
- Compactly visualizing relations of a large system whose system relations cannot be represented by a diagram on a single sheet of paper due to the size of the system.
- Studying relations from a particular scope and type of element by filtering the unimportant data. Hierarchical columns allow for browsing directly in the created matrix through the model scope and visualize the required area.
- Showing relations that cannot be represented on diagrams, such as a representation (a class by the lifeline), a behavior representation in other diagrams, an operation representation by Call Behavior Action, Use Case relations with describing activities through the property Owned Behavior, etc. The semantic dependency matrix is needed for deeper model analysis. The Dependency Matrix allows the representation of any kind of relations through element properties.

To learn about Dependency Matrix properties, please click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating new dependency matrix type' space='MD2024x'].

308903550

## PAGE 00424: Dependency Matrix Criteria area

- page_id: `136709929`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709929/Dependency+Matrix+Criteria+area
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:15.803+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Analysis diagrams > Dependency Matrix > Dependency Matrix environment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To select the specified value in the Model Browser or to open its Specification window, you can right-click on the value boxes (except for the Dependency Criteria) in the **Criteria** area.

See the descriptions of the dependency matrix Criteria area in the following table.

| Element Name | Element Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Row Element Type | Text box with | Click the ... button to select an element type or multiple element types to show in rows of the dependency matrix. |
| Column Element Type | Text box with | Click the ... button to select an element or multiple element types to show in columns of the dependency matrix. |
| Row Scope | Text box with | Click the ... button to define a scope of the model (packages/profiles) from which elements should be displayed in rows of the dependency matrix.Click to to open the Query dialog and specify the criteria that define row scope. |
| Column Scope | Text box with | Click the ... button to define a scope of the model (packages/profiles) from which elements should be displayed in columns of the dependency matrix.Click to to open the Query dialog and specify the criteria that define column scope. |
| Dependency Criteria | Text box with | Click the ... button to define what relationships between row and column elements you wish to display in the dependency matrix cells. |
| Direction | Drop-down list | Select a direction of relationships for the relationship analysis and representation in the dependency matrix. Be aware that new relationships will be created with the selected direction. |
| Show Elements | Drop-down list | Select to show only related (by a selected dependency criteria), only non-related, or all elements. |

## PAGE 00426: Dependency Matrix shortcut menu

- page_id: `136709932`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709932/Dependency+Matrix+shortcut+menu
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:16.226+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Analysis diagrams > Dependency Matrix > Dependency Matrix environment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To open the dependency matrix shortcut menu

- Right-click an empty space on one of the following [ATTACHMENT filename='Dep_matrix_shortcut_menu.png']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space='MD2024x']
  - Matrix pane

| Command | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Specification (Enter) | Opens the of a dependency matrix. |
| Go To | Navigates back or forward to the diagram that was open in the current diagram tab, finds and navigates to diagrams wherein the matrix is used, or to the matrix owner in the , or to a hyperlinked object, or to an element to which the selected element is related through a traceability relationship. |
| Select in Containment Tree (Alt+B) | Selects the dependency matrix in the of the . |
| Related Elements | Allows selecting whether to create a for the matrix or search for model elements which depends on the matrix or are used by the matrix. |
| Refactor | the matrix to a diagram indicated by the user. |
| Tools | Opens a list of that are available for the matrix. |
| Print Active Diagram | Prints a matrix. |
| Show Tabs in Full Screen (F11) | Shows a matrix in the full screen mode. Click Close Full Screen to return to the previous view. |
| Close Tab (Ctrl+F4) | Closes an active matrix. |
| Close All Tabs But Current (Ctrl+Shift+F4) | Closes all diagrams except an active one. |
| Close All Tabs(Ctrl+Alt+F4) | Closes all opened diagrams. |

1352667888

1352667888

The Dependency Matrix tool allows templates to:

- Access data from Dependency Matrix.
- Use diagrams to get data from Dependency Matrix.
- Get row nodes and elements.
- Get column nodes and elements.
- Get dependencies in a cell (between specific row and column nodes).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The Dependency Matrix diagram.

Like other Custom tools, the Generic Table tool '*dependencymatrixtool.jar*' must be presented in the 'extensions' folder of the Report Wizard plugin. For further information on Custom tools and the installation, see section *[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom tool' space='MD2024x']*.

To import the Dependency Matrix tool to a template, type the following code in the template:

```text

```

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714497 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00428: Dependency matrix tool API

- page_id: `136714497`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714497/Dependency+matrix+tool+API
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:38.092+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Dependency Matrix tool
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1352819143

1352819146

1352819140

**On this page**

4

1352819146

Dependency Matrix tool allows templates to access data from a Dependency Matrix, use diagrams to get data, row elements, column names, or relations between row and column elements from the matrix.

You can use the following Dependency Matrix tool API in the template.

**Class DependencyMatrixTool**

Use the following methods to use diagrams to get data.

- +getMatrix( diagram : Diagram) : Matrix
- +getMatrix( diagramName : String) : Matrix

**Class Matrix**

Use the following methods to get matrix row elements or nodes, column elements or nodes, and dependencies between these nodes/elements.

- +getRowNodes() : List<ElementNode>
- +getColumnNodes() : List<ElementNode>
- +getCellDependencies( rowNode: ElementNode, columnNode: ElementNode)
- +getRowElements() : List<Element>
- +getColumnElements() : List<Element>
- +getRelation( row: Element, column : Element) : List<Relation>

**Class Relation**

Describes dependency in a Dependency Matrix cell. Use the following methods for class relations.

- +getName() : String
- +getElement() : Element
- +getSemanticType() : String
- +getDirection() : String

##### Using Diagrams to Get Data from Dependency Matrix

A Dependency Matrix is a special diagram. You can retrieve it using the $Diagram variable. Thus, every method must accept a diagram instance or a diagram’s name. Use the following methods to return a Dependency Matrix instance. You can retrieve rows and columns from the Dependency Matrix instances.

##### Getting Dependency Matrix Instances from Diagram Elements

**getMatrix(diagram : Diagram) : Matrix** 
To get a Dependency Matrix instance from a specified diagram element, use the following code.

```text

```

Where the parameter is:

- diagram – a diagram element

Return an instance of Dependency Matrix.

For example:

```text

```

##### Getting Dependency Matrix Instances from Diagram Names

**getMatrix(diagramName : String) : Matrix** 
To get a Dependency Matrix instance from a specified diagram’s name.

```text

```

Where the parameter is:

- diagram – a diagram’s name

Return a Dependency Matrix instance.

Example code:

```text

```

##### Getting Rows and Columns

The Matrix consists of rows and columns. Each row or column is an instance of ElementNode object. Each ElementNode represents a single model Element. There might be multiple ElementNodes for the same model Element in a Dependency Matrix diagram, since the same model Element can be represented multiple times in a diagram.

##### Getting All Rows

**getRowNodes() : List<ElementNode>**

Use this method to retrieve a list of rows, ordered in the same way as actually displayed in the diagram. Nodes representing grouping packages, that do not belong to the actual matrix data, are excluded.

```text

```

The returned value is a list of ElementNodes. Method **getElement() : Element** can then be used to retrieve the model element represented by a row.

To print all row elements' names, for example, type the following code:

```text

```

##### Getting all Columns

**getColumnNodes() : List<ElementNode>**

Use this method to retrieve a list of columns, ordered in the same way as actually displayed in the diagram. Nodes representing grouping packages, that do not belong to the actual matrix data, are excluded.

```text

```

The returned value is a list of ElementNodes. Method **getElement() : Element** can then be used to retrieve the model element represented by a column.

To print all column elements' names, for example, type the following code:

```text

```

##### Getting Dependencies between Rows and Columns

**getCellDependencies(rowNode: ElementNode, columnNode: ElementNode)**

Use this method to retrieve the relations in a specific matrix cell, which is identified by provided row and column nodes.

```text

```

Where the parameter is:

- rowNode – ElementNode of a row
- columnNode – ElementNode of a column

The returned value is a list of Relations.

The Relation class contains the following methods:

- getSemanticType : String Return the semantic type
- getElement : Element Return a relationship element or null if the relationship is not an element, for example, tag name.
- getName : String Return a relationship name. If the relationship is a tag name, it will return the tag name. If the relationship is NamedElement, it will return the element name; otherwise, return a human name.
- getDirection : String Return the direction.

To print the row, column, and its relationship name, for example, type the following code:

```text

```

To print rows, columns, and relations in a spreadsheet file format, for example, type the following code:

| #import('depmatrix','com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.DependencyMatrixTool')#forpage ($diagram in $report.filterDiagram($Diagram,["Dependency Matrix"]))#set($matrix=$depmatrix.getMatrix($diagram))$bookmark.create($diagram.name) |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | #forcol ($col in $matrix.columnNodes)$col.element.name#endcol |  |
| #forrow($row in $matrix.rowNodes)$row.element.name | #forcol ($col in $matrix.columns)#if(!$report.isEmpty($matrix.getCellDependencies($row,$col)))->#end#endcol | #endrow |
| #endpage |  |  |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d65960db-9283-4000-b9b5-50182873066d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1352819143</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="051b0d55-0930-4a8b-a13e-a2c93806dcc7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1352819146</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9090caa7-1df9-4465-b750-53f7a15bfa70"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1352819140</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="84dddb78-0faa-4e33-a4a2-c2b3cb578630"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="88281c9b-9444-466f-8a45-e22db9b7496c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1352819146</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Dependency Matrix tool allows templates to access data from a Dependency Matrix, use diagrams to get data, row elements, column names, or relations between row and column elements from the matrix.</p><p>You can use the following Dependency Matrix tool API in the template.</p><p><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="42219764-7874-4d90-8605-96c5f23289a9">Class DependencyMatrixTool</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong></p><p>Use the following methods to use diagrams to get data.</p><ul><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getMatrix( diagram : Diagram) : Matrix</li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getMatrix( diagramName : String) : Matrix <br /><br /></li></ul><p><strong>Class Matrix</strong></p><p>Use the following methods to get matrix row elements or nodes, column elements or nodes, and dependencies between these nodes/elements.</p><ul><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getRowNodes() : List&lt;ElementNode&gt;</li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getColumnNodes() : List&lt;ElementNode&gt;</li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getCellDependencies( rowNode: ElementNode, columnNode: ElementNode)</li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getRowElements() : List&lt;Element&gt;</li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getColumnElements() : List&lt;Element&gt; </li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getRelation( row: Element, column : Element) : List&lt;Relation&gt; <br /><br /></li></ul><p><strong>Class Relation</strong></p><p>Describes dependency in a Dependency Matrix cell. Use the following methods for class relations.</p><ul><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getName() : String</li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getElement() : Element </li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getSemanticType() : String </li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;">+getDirection() : String</li></ul><h3>Using Diagrams to Get Data from Dependency Matrix</h3><p>A Dependency Matrix is a special diagram. You can retrieve it using the $Diagram variable. Thus, every method must accept a diagram instance or a diagram’s name. Use the following methods to return a Dependency Matrix instance. You can retrieve rows and columns from the Dependency Matrix instances.</p><h3>Getting Dependency Matrix Instances from Diagram Elements</h3><p><strong>getMatrix(diagram : Diagram) : Matrix</strong><br />To get a Dependency Matrix instance from a specified diagram element, use the following code.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8fb3ec99-7085-4d08-a2ce-b6ee4e90c5aa"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set($matrix = $depmatrix.getMatrix($diagram))]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Where the parameter is:</p><ul><li>diagram – a diagram element</li></ul><p>Return an instance of Dependency Matrix.</p><p>For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d1eeac84-576f-424b-a258-933e831a39f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($diagram in $project.getDiagrams("Dependency Matrix"))
#set($matrix = $depmatrix.getMatrix($diagram)) 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Getting Dependency Matrix Instances from Diagram Names</h3><p><strong>getMatrix(diagramName : String) : Matrix</strong> <br />To get a Dependency Matrix instance from a specified diagram’s name.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="076989fa-ed89-4186-9c35-315bb3c52b5f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set($matrix = $depmatrix.getMatrix($diagram))]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Where the parameter is: </p><ul><li>diagram – a diagram’s name</li></ul><p>Return a Dependency Matrix instance.</p><p>Example code:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2cb801ad-449e-4a77-8e55-9262a5d0f37b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set($diagram = "diagram name")
#set($matrix = $depmatrix.getMatrix($diagram))]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Getting Rows and Columns</h3><p>The Matrix consists of rows and columns. Each row or column is an instance of ElementNode object. Each ElementNode represents a single model Element. There might be multiple ElementNodes for the same model Element in a Dependency Matrix diagram, since the same model Element can be represented multiple times in a diagram.</p><h3>Getting All Rows</h3><p><strong><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">getRowNodes() : List&lt;ElementNode&gt;</span></strong></p><p>Use this method to retrieve a list of rows, ordered in the same way as actually displayed in the diagram. Nodes representing grouping packages, that do not belong to the actual matrix data, are excluded.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0a742c90-fbaa-40f9-8fc1-fc0ab63d716a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$matrix.getRowNodes()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The returned value is a list of ElementNodes. Method <strong>getElement() : Element</strong> can then be used to retrieve the model element represented by a row.</p><p>To print all row elements' names, for example, type the following code:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="65eabb1b-3fbe-49bd-bbc9-a58dd91223f1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($rowNode in $matrix.rowNodes) 
$rowNode.element.name 
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Getting all Columns</h3><p><strong>getColumnNodes() : List&lt;ElementNode&gt;</strong></p><p>Use this method to retrieve a list of columns, ordered in the same way as actually displayed in the diagram. Nodes representing grouping packages, that do not belong to the actual matrix data, are excluded.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="778b1227-f91a-4dca-929f-7e764a388888"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$matrix.getRowNodes()]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The returned value is a list of ElementNodes. Method <strong>getElement() : Element</strong> can then be used to retrieve the model element represented by a column.</p><p>To print all column elements' names, for example, type the following code:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b930c55b-1af1-4d2d-a759-56410c12be65"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($col in $matrix.columns) 
$colNode.element.name
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Getting Dependencies between Rows and Columns</h3><p><strong>getCellDependencies(rowNode: ElementNode, columnNode: ElementNode)</strong></p><p>Use this method to retrieve the relations in a specific matrix cell, which is identified by provided row and column nodes.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f3cfac07-2d4a-4984-9227-d082e318c001"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$matrix.getCellDependencies($rowNode, $columnNode)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Where the parameter is:</p><ul><li>rowNode –  ElementNode of a row</li><li>columnNode –  ElementNode of a column</li></ul><p>The returned value is a list of Relations.</p><p>The Relation class contains the following methods:</p><ul><li><strong>getSemanticType</strong> : String<br />             Return the semantic type</li><li><strong>getElement</strong> : Element <br />             Return a relationship element or null if the relationship is not an element, for example, tag name.</li><li><strong>getName</strong> : String <br />             Return a relationship name. If the relationship is a tag name, it will return the tag name. If the relationship is NamedElement, it will return the element name;              otherwise, return a human name.</li><li><strong>getDirection</strong> : String <br />             Return the direction.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To print the row, column, and its relationship name, for example, type the following code:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="722e0feb-cfdd-4eea-b81e-ddf75989285c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($row in $matrix.rowNodes) 
   #foreach($col in $matrix.columnNodes)
      #foreach($rel in $matrix.getCellDependencies($row, $col))
         $row.element.name has $rel.name with $col.element.name
      #end
   #end
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>To print rows, columns, and relations in a spreadsheet file format, for example, type the following code:</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 90.779%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 53.1113%;" /><col style="width: 40.3155%;" /><col style="width: 6.57318%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><pre>#import('depmatrix','com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.DependencyMatrixTool')</pre><pre>#forpage ($diagram in $report.filterDiagram($Diagram,[&quot;Dependency Matrix&quot;]))</pre><pre>#set($matrix=$depmatrix.getMatrix($diagram))</pre><pre>$bookmark.create($diagram.name)</pre></td><td><br /></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td colspan="1"><br /></td><td class="highlight-grey" colspan="1" data-highlight-colour="grey"><pre>#forcol ($col in $matrix.columnNodes)$col.element.name#endcol</pre></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr><tr><td class="highlight-grey" data-highlight-colour="grey"><pre><br /></pre><pre><br /></pre><pre>#forrow($row in $matrix.rowNodes)$row.element.name</pre></td><td><pre>#forcol ($col in </pre><pre>$matrix.columns)#if(!$report.isEmpty($matrix.getCellDependencies($row,$col)))</pre><pre>-&gt;#end#endcol</pre></td><td colspan="1"><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><pre>#endrow</pre></td></tr><tr><td><pre>#endpage</pre></td><td><br /></td><td colspan="1"><br /></td></tr></tbody></table></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709923 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00429: Dependency Matrix toolbar

- page_id: `136709923`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709923/Dependency+Matrix+toolbar
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:15.363+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Analysis diagrams > Dependency Matrix > Dependency Matrix environment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

See the detailed descriptions of the dependency matrix toolbar buttons in the following table.

| Button | Button name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Back (Alt+Left) | Navigates back to the previous diagram that was open in the current diagram tab. |
|  | Forward (Atl+Right) | Navigates forward to the diagram in the current diagram tab. |
|  | Select in Containment Tree (Alt+B) | Opens the in the and selects the current diagram or symbol in the Containment tree. |
|  | Delete (Ctrl+D) | Deletes the selected element from the matrix and model.If you undo the deletion, you should refresh the matrix to make the deleted elements appear in the matrix again. |
|  | Remove From Matrix (Delete) | Removes the selected element from the matrix. The selected element is not removed from the model. |
|  | Change Axes | Transposes rows and columns. |
|  | Export | Exports a matrix as a Comma Separated Values ( .csv) file. The file can be opened with Microsoft Excel. |
|  | Refresh | Refreshes the matrix after updating the criteria or data. |
|  | Validate Diagram | Validates the matrix against all validation rules stored in the model, except those that are ignored. |
|  | Options | Expands the menu with other options. |
|  | Specification - opens the matrix Specification window wherein you can specify matrix properties.Legend Location - lists legend location options and selection to not show it.Include Additional Content - includes additional content of elements that fall within the specified row/column scope. For example, if a Smart Package comes within the scope of the dependency matrix, its content is collected and represented in the matrix. |  |
|  | Suppress/Expand Criteria Area | Suppresses or expands the matrix Criteria area. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

## PAGE 00430: Deployment

- page_id: `136710875`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710875/Deployment
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:04.135+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Artifact
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

907499974

907499976

907499975

The Deployment Specification is a type of Artifact. The Deployment Specification is a general mechanism to parameterize a Deployment relationship.

To draw the Deployment Specification on the diagram pane

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component diagram' space='MD2024x'] (or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deployment diagram' space='MD2024x'] ) diagram toolbar, click the Deployment Diagram button group to access the drop-down menu.
2. In the opened list, select Deployment Specification Instance and draw it on your diagram pane.

To specify the Deployment relationship

1. Create the Deployment Specification on the diagram pane.
2. Between the Deployment relationship and Deployment Specification, draw the Dependency relationship. The Dependency is drawn without an arrow. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2017-06-23 at 5.52.38 PM.png']

907499973

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deployment diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Artifacts' space='CBMP2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with dependencies' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ed6abe49-47b5-4303-a98f-dfa3bae2e6ce"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">907499974</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="10b6bbe8-4369-4129-9da3-fe4b146ac880"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">907499976</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bb9eb142-3e5f-407a-9440-db78a9e17973"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">907499975</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Deployment Specification is a type of Artifact. The Deployment Specification is a general mechanism to parameterize a Deployment relationship.</p><p>To draw the Deployment Specification on the diagram pane</p><hr /><ol><li>In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Component diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Component]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deployment diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Deployment]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>) diagram toolbar, click the Deployment Diagram button group to access the drop-down menu. </li><li>In the opened list, select Deployment Specification Instance and draw it on your diagram pane.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To specify the Deployment relationship</p><hr /><ol><li>Create the Deployment Specification on the diagram pane. </li><li>Between the Deployment relationship and Deployment Specification, draw the Dependency relationship. The Dependency is drawn without an arrow.<br /><ac:image ac:height="250"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2017-06-23 at 5.52.38 PM.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deployment" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f39c902b-0523-4907-ba09-5f5b22fb1fe7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">907499973</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Component diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deployment diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CBMP2024x" ri:content-title="Artifacts" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Artifact]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Working with dependencies" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Working with Dependencies]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Dependency" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709808 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00431: Deployment diagram

- page_id: `136709808`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709808/Deployment+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:08.880+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > UML diagrams
- labels: ['implementation', 'implementation-diagram']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308620457

308620459

308620458

**Overview**

A Deployment diagram falls under the structural diagramming family. It focuses on showing the physical layout of various hardware components ([CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Node' space='MD2024x']), e.g. CPU, a database server, and an application server that compose a system as well as the distribution of executable programs (software components) on hardware. For instance, to describe a website, the Deployment diagram would show what hardware components exist, what software components run, and how the different pieces are interconnected. Deployment diagrams are crucial when dealing with distributed systems.

As of MagicDraw 17.0.1, the Deployment diagram (or the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component diagram' space='MD2024x']) replaces the Implementation diagram, which is no longer supported in UML standard. An Implementation diagram created with earlier versions of MagicDraw, now opens as

- Deployment diagram, if Nodes were used in the Implementation diagram
- Component diagram, if Nodes were not used in the Implementation diagram

Customized diagrams that were based on the Implementation diagram are now based on the Component diagram.

**Purpose**

Using a Deployment diagram, you can show the actual computers and devices (Nodes), along with the connections they have to each other, thus specifying a system topology. Inside the Nodes, executable [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component' space='MD2024x'] and objects are located in a way that it shows where the software units are residing and on which Nodes they are executed. You may also show dependencies between Components.

**Usage**

A Deployment diagram can be used to:

- show the structure of the run-time system
- capture the hardware that is going to be used to implement the system and links between different hardware items
- model physical hardware elements and the communication paths between them
- plan the system architecture
- document the deployment of software components or nodes.

**Summary**

Deployment diagrams are valuable because they can be:

- used to model the hardware platform for a system
- used to identify hardware capabilities that have an impact on the performance planning and software configuration.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Deployment diagram

308620456

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='MD2024x']

An Implementation diagram created with earlier versions of MagicDraw, now opens as</p><ul><li>Deployment diagram, if Nodes were used in the Implementation diagram</li><li>Component diagram, if Nodes were not used in the Implementation diagram</li></ul><p>Customized diagrams that were based on the Implementation diagram are now based on the Component diagram.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><strong>Purpose</strong></p><p>Using a Deployment diagram, you can show the actual computers and devices (Nodes), along with the connections they have to each other, thus specifying a system topology. You may also show dependencies between Components.</p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>A Deployment diagram can be used to:</p><ul><li>show the structure of the run-time system</li><li>capture the hardware that is going to be used to implement the system and links between different hardware items</li><li>model physical hardware elements and the communication paths between them</li><li>plan the system architecture</li><li>document the deployment of software components or nodes. </li></ul><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Deployment diagrams are valuable because they can be:</p><ul><li>used to model the hardware platform for a system</li><li>used to identify hardware capabilities that have an impact on the performance planning and software configuration. </li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="deployment_diagram_example.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deployment diagram" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a Deployment diagram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="292ab185-bc83-4d52-bf5f-8dc8aad28c4b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308620456</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00432: Deprecated JavaScript

- page_id: `136712792`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712792/Deprecated+JavaScript
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:53.390+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Validation > UML validation rules > Deprecated JavaScript rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

JS

**Description**

This validation rule checks if the specified expression does not use Javascript Nashorn scripting language that is deprecated.

**Severity**

error

**Constrained Element**

Element

**Solvers**

- Change to JavaScript Rhino - changes the Javascript Nashorn scripting language to JavaScript Rhino.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *Parameter Direction and Type*Generic Table has a custom Column *Parameter Direction and Type using StringConcat* that implements a Javascript Nashorn script. 
The error is resolved by changing the scripting language to JavaScript Rhino via the **Change to JavaScript Rhino** solver.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>JS</p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if the specified expression does not use Javascript Nashorn scripting language that is deprecated.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>error</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Element</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><ul><li><strong>Change to JavaScript Rhino</strong> - changes the Javascript Nashorn scripting language to JavaScript Rhino.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Deprecated JavaScript.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Deprecated JavaScript" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The <em>Parameter Direction and Type </em>Generic Table has a custom Column <em>Parameter Direction and Type using StringConcat</em> that implements a Javascript Nashorn script.<br />The error is resolved by changing the scripting language to JavaScript Rhino via the <strong>Change to JavaScript Rhino</strong> solver.</h6><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136727575 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00433: Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn

- page_id: `136727575`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136727575/Deprecated+JavaScript+Nashorn
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-22T06:43:10.311+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation > Supported scripting languages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

965417895

INLINE

965417916

INLINE

965417905

INLINE

The JavaScript Nashorn language has been removed from the modeling tool. You can learn more about deprecated JavaScript Nashorn [here](https://openjdk.java.net/jeps/335). Please use JavaScript Rhino instead. For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='JavaScript migration from Nashorn to Rhino engine' space=''] page.

All JavaScript Nashorn usages need to be changed to JavaScript Rhino in projects to work correctly. Below are the recommended steps for achieving that:

1. Use one of the following options to run the modeling tool:
  - Proceeding with this option means that none of your project's JavaScript Nashorn scripts will work until they are changed to or rewritten in JavaScript Rhino.
  - Proceeding with this option means that your project's JavaScript Nashorn scripts will be executed using the JavaScript Rhino engine; thus, the majority of the scripts will work, but they will still need to be changed to or rewritten in JavaScript Rhino.
2. 
3. 

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Diagram showing the JavaScript Nashorn usages' change to JavaScript Rhino.

##### Running the modeling tool

###### Option 1. Run modeling tool without executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts (default)

Following this option means:

- The JavaScript Nashorn scripts found in the project are not executed, meaning that the model will not function as expected due to script failures.
- This is the default option; simply run the modeling tool and open a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, an 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification is displayed.

###### Option 2. Run modeling tool executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts with JavaScript Rhino engine

Following this option means:

- The JavaScript Nashorn scripts found in the project are executed using the JavaScript Rhino engine. Due to the similarities between Rhino and Nashorn, most of the project's scripts are likely to work.
- To proceed with this option, you need to add an additional system property, as described below.

To enable JavaScript Nashorn script execution with JavaScript Rhino engine

1. Open the <modeling tool installation directory>\bin\<modeling tool name>.properties file.
2. Locate the JAVA_ARGS= line
3. Add the following system property: -Dexecute.legacy.javascript.as.rhino=true
4. Restart the modeling tool.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, the 'JavaScript Nashorn (deprecated)' error notification is displayed.

##### Opening a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages

Opening a project that contains JavaScript *Nashorn* scripts immediately displays a notification about the removal of the JavaScript *Nashorn* scripting language. The notification is displayed only once upon the first opening of the project with the new version of the tool. You can click the **Find Usages**link to run the **Deprecated JavaScript** validation suite to locate all usages of JavaScript Nashorn (for more information, see the section below)*.*

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### **Finding and resolving JavaScript Nashorn**usages**

The recommended steps to find and resolve JavaScript Nashorn usages:

1. Run the Deprecated JavaScript validation suite to .
2. Evaluate each script failing the validation and rewrite it in JavaScript Rhino if necessary. Due to the similarities between JavaScript Rhino and JavaScript Nashorn, most of the project's Nashorn scripts are likely to work with the Rhino engine. However, a review is recommended as to whether the Nashorn scripts' execution with the Rhino engine are accurate. If not, the Nashorn script must be modified to work in Rhino.
3. via the **Change to JavaScript Rhino**solver.

###### Finding JavaScript Nashorn**usages

To locate all usages of JavaScript Nashorn in a project, we recommend running the**Deprecated JavaScript**validation suite (**Analyze**>**Validation**>**Validate**). The validation suite contains the **Deprecated JavaScript** validation rule, which:

- detects all JavaScript Nashorn**usages;
- allows you to change the JavaScript Nashorn**language to JavaScript Rhino**for implemented expressions via the **Change to JavaScript Rhino**solver.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### After running the **Deprecated JavaScript** validation suite, hold Ctrl+A to select all detected instances of JavaScript *Nashorn* and resolve them via the **Change to JavaScript Rhino** solver in the Validation Results panel.

For a detailed description of the validation rule, see the **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deprecated JavaScript' space='']** page.

###### Resolving JavaScript Nashorn**usages

The **Change to JavaScript Rhino**solver changes the used scripting language without affecting the script itself. In many cases, changing the used scripting language will not affect the functionality of the script. Otherwise, the 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification (shown below) will appear, requiring you to rewrite the script in JavaScript Rhino.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Run modeling tool without executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts (default)</h4><p>Following this option means:</p><ul><li>The JavaScript Nashorn scripts found in the project are not executed, meaning that the model will not function as expected due to script failures.</li><li>This is the default option; simply run the modeling tool and open a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages.</li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="expression_evaluation_error.png" /></ac:image>For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, an 'Expression evaluation failure' error notification is displayed.</h6><h4>Option 2. Run modeling tool executing JavaScript Nashorn scripts with JavaScript Rhino engine</h4><p>Following this option means:</p><ul><li>The JavaScript Nashorn scripts found in the project are executed using the JavaScript Rhino engine. Due to the similarities between Rhino and Nashorn, most of the project's scripts are likely to work.</li><li>To proceed with this option, you need to add an additional system property, as described below.</li></ul><p>To enable JavaScript Nashorn script execution with JavaScript Rhino engine</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\bin\&lt;modeling tool name&gt;.properties</em> file.</li><li>Locate the <em>JAVA_ARGS=</em> line</li><li>Add the following system property:<br /><em>-Dexecute.legacy.javascript.as.rhino=true</em></li><li><span>Restart the modeling tool.</span></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Deprecated_Nashorn_error.png" /></ac:image>For example, if you open a table containing a custom column that has an expression with a JavaScript Nashorn script, the 'JavaScript Nashorn (deprecated)' error notification is displayed.</h6><h3>Opening a project containing JavaScript Nashorn usages</h3><p>Opening a project that contains JavaScript <em>Nashorn</em> scripts immediately displays a notification about the removal of the JavaScript <em>Nashorn</em> scripting language. The notification is displayed only once upon the first opening of the project with the new version of the tool. You can click the <strong>Find Usages </strong>link to run the <strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong> validation suite to locate all usages of JavaScript Nashorn (for more information, see the <ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Finding and resolving JavaScript Nashorn usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> section below)<em>.</em></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Discontinued_Nashorn_notification.png" /></ac:image></p><h3><strong>Finding and resolving <span>JavaScript Nashorn<em> </em>usages</span></strong></h3><p>The recommended steps to find and resolve JavaScript Nashorn usages:</p><ol><li class="auto-cursor-target">Run the <strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong><span> validation suite to <ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[find all JavaScript Nashorn usages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li class="auto-cursor-target"><p>Evaluate each script failing the validation and rewrite it in JavaScript Rhino if necessary.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00e77b0b-72b5-4a81-87c2-56b74adc6f65"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Due to the similarities between JavaScript Rhino and JavaScript Nashorn, most of the project's Nashorn scripts are likely to work with the Rhino engine. If not, the Nashorn script must be modified to work in Rhino.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Resolve the validation error]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> via the <span><strong style="text-align: left;">Change to JavaScript Rhino </strong>solver.</span></li></ol><h4>Finding JavaScript Nashorn<em> </em>usages</h4><p class="auto-cursor-target">To locate all <span>usages of JavaScript Nashorn in a project, we recommend running the </span><strong>Deprecated JavaScript</strong><span> validation suite (</span><strong>Analyze</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>Validation</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>Validate</strong><span>). <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715042 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00435: Derived properties visibility

- page_id: `136715042`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715042/Derived+properties+visibility
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:01.610+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data > Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1852208784

1852208787

1852208785

A newly created derived property is visible by default in the following places:

- General specification pane of the DSL element’s [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] .
- Properties panel of the DSL element.
- **Compartment Edit** dialog. To see derived properties of customized elements that have the**Used UML Properties** property specified, ensure that derived properties are assigned as values for the **Used UML Properties** property.
- **Criterion Editor** dialog for editing relation criteria in a Relation Map diagram.
- **Select Dependency Criteria** dialog for editing the dependency criteria in a Dependency Matrix 
diagram.

Additionally, DSL customization allows more precision when specifying a derived property’s visibility, as this can be done through the specification of the DSL element’s properties group or subgroup to which the derived property is assigned. As a result, the derived property will be visible in the group or subgroup according to this group’s or subgroup’s visibility settings.

To make derived properties of customized elements having the Used UML Properties property specified visible

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] of the customization element.
2. In the general specification pane, select Used UML Properties and assign one or more derived property values.
3. Save and reopen the project.

To assign the derived property to a property group

1. Select the customization element with the derived property.
2. Create a property group for the customization element.
3. In the general specification pane, edit property values that specify the property group’s visibility.
4. In the property group [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] , click the Properties property and then click the ... button.
5. In the Item Filter dialog, select the check box near the appropriate derived property and click OK . As shown in the following figure, the selected derived property will be assigned to Properties as a new value.
6. Click Close .

Use a similar procedure to assign the derived property to a property subgroup. Create a property subgroup in addition and assign the derived property to the subgroup, rather than the property group.

You can also assign a derived property to a property group not specified in the same customization element if both customization elements extend the same UML element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Derived property assigned to property group.

All derived properties, together with custom UML properties, are available for creating your own Report templates.

1852208783

**Related Pages**

- [Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Extending+Metamodel+with+Derived+Properties)
- [Using Customization Data](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Using+Customization+Data)
- [Creating Customization Data](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+Customization+Data)
- [Creating Property Groups and Subgroups](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+Property+Groups+and+Subgroups)

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7528d95b-4c3e-4c7d-8334-ceff8b8920a2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852208784</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8a632f58-0b1a-43fd-9d91-0e06cb4654f1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852208787</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3d21a2a2-d4d8-4837-b131-71c8f3e00902"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852208785</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>A newly created derived property is visible by default in the following places:</p><ul><li>General specification pane of the DSL element’s <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</li><li><strong>Properties</strong> panel of the DSL element.</li><li><p><strong>Compartment Edit</strong> dialog. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="382e662a-ac44-4527-9565-37b7cede5b54"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To see derived properties of customized elements that have the<strong> Used UML Properties</strong> property specified, ensure that derived properties are assigned as values for the <strong>Used UML Properties</strong> property.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li><p><strong>Criterion Editor</strong> dialog for editing relation criteria in a Relation Map diagram.</p></li><li><p><strong>Select Dependency Criteria</strong> dialog for editing the dependency criteria in a Dependency Matrix<br />diagram.</p></li></ul><p>Additionally, DSL customization allows more precision when specifying a derived property’s visibility, as this can be done through the specification of the DSL element’s properties group or subgroup to which the derived property is assigned. As a result, the derived property will be visible in the group or subgroup according to this group’s or subgroup’s visibility settings.</p><p><br /></p><p>To make derived properties of customized elements having the Used UML Properties property specified visible</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> of the customization element.</li><li>In the general specification pane, select <strong>Used UML Properties</strong> and assign one or more derived property values.</li><li>Save and reopen the project.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To assign the derived property to a property group</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the customization element with the derived property.</li><li>Create a property group for the customization element.</li><li>In the general specification pane, edit property values that specify the property group’s visibility.</li><li>In the property group <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>, click the <strong>Properties</strong> property and then click the <strong>...</strong> button.</li><li>In the <strong>Item Filter</strong> dialog, select the check box near the appropriate derived property and click <strong>OK</strong>. As shown in the following figure, the selected derived property will be assigned to <strong>Properties</strong> as a new value.</li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ol><p>Use a similar procedure to assign the derived property to a property subgroup. Create a property subgroup in addition and assign the derived property to the subgroup, rather than the property group.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae27718d-8b3a-457c-abe3-43e77f38ccbb"><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also assign a derived property to a property group not specified in the same customization element if both customization elements extend the same UML element.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Derived property assigned to property group" ac:alt="Derived property assigned to property group"><ri:attachment ri:filename="deriver_property_tgs.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Derived properties visibility" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Derived property assigned to property group.</h6><p>All derived properties, together with custom UML properties, are available for creating your own Report templates.</p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fa80ee33-5047-4fa0-9c64-0a7b63221206"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1852208783</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Extending+Metamodel+with+Derived+Properties">Extending Metamodel with Derived Properties</a></span></li><li class="ancestor-link"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Using+Customization+Data">Using Customization Data</a></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+Customization+Data">Creating Customization Data</a></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Creating+Property+Groups+and+Subgroups">Creating Property Groups and Subgroups</a> </span></li></ul><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715053 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00437: Developer Guide

- page_id: `136715053`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715053/Developer+Guide
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:05:25.438+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In the generated javadoc ([https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/](https://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/)), you will find a detailed description of classes, their attributes, and operations. JavaDocs are located in *<modeling tool installation directory>\openapi\docs*.

This guide describes the Open Java API of MagicDraw or a Cameo Suite product, such as Cameo Systems Modeler, and provides instructions on how to implement custom plugins, add actions to the menus or toolbars, change UML model elements as well as create new patterns. Though MagicDraw is used in examples mainly, other modeling tools, since they are based on the MagicDraw modeling platform, perform the same.

The program core code and used libraries are packaged in jars files that are located in *<modeling tool installation directory>\lib* (and its subdirectories).

Some plugins also exposes Open API. Plugins libraries can be located in *<modeling tool installation directory>\plugins\<plugin_name>*

The code can be divided into the following three API scope groups:

- OpenAPI – a code for public usage, stable through builds and versions.
- InternalAPI – a code for the internal NoMagic usage only, may change through builds and versions without any restrictions.
- NotAPI – not an API code, it may change in each build.

Java annotations are used to distinguish which API scope in the provided code belongs to. Annotations of the type (class, interface, enum) declaration:

| Annotation | API Scope | Scope applies to |
| --- | --- | --- |
| @OpenApiAll | OpenAPI | The annotated type declaration and all declared members, constructors. |
| @OpenApi | OpenAPI | The annotated type declaration only. Each declared member, constructor is annotated explicitly with the @OpenApi or @InternalApi annotation (see the following table). |
| @InternalApi | InternalAPI | The annotated type declaration and all declared members, constructors. |
| @NotApi | NotAPI | The annotated type declaration and all declared members, constructors. |

Annotations of the member, constructor declaration of the @OpenApi type:

| Annotation | API Scope | Scope applies to |
| --- | --- | --- |
| @OpenApi | OpenAPI | The annotated declared member or constructor. |
| @InternalApi | InternalAPI | The annotated declared member or constructor. |

Only code from the OpenAPI scope should be used to extend the modeling tool. All the InternalAPI and NotAPI scope code are additionally annotated with the *@Deprecated* annotation. This allows seeing the illegal API usage in all modern IDEs without an additional configuration.

We provide a set of plugin samples in *<*modeling tool installation directory*>\openapi\examples*. By using these examples, you can find out on how to use an OpenAPI.

In the generated javadoc, you will find the detailed description of classes, their attributes, and operations. JavaDocs are located in *<*modeling tool installation directory*>\openapi\docs*.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

JavaDocs are located in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\openapi\docs</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>This guide describes the Open Java API of MagicDraw or a Cameo Suite product, such as Cameo Systems Modeler, and provides instructions on how to implement custom plugins, add actions to the menus or toolbars, change UML model elements as well as create new patterns. Though MagicDraw is used in examples mainly, other modeling tools, since they are based on the MagicDraw modeling platform, perform the same. </p><p>The program core code and used libraries are packaged in jars files that are located in <em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="591a288f-54a7-41e2-b270-13fecabdf158">&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;</ac:inline-comment-marker>\lib</em> (and its subdirectories).</p><p>Some plugins also exposes Open API. Plugins libraries can be located in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\plugins\&lt;plugin_name&gt;</em></p><p> The code can be divided into the following three API scope groups:</p><ul><li><strong>OpenAPI </strong>– a code for public usage, stable through builds and versions.</li><li><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">InternalAPI </strong>– a code for the internal NoMagic usage only, may change through builds and versions without any restrictions.</li><li><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">NotAPI </strong>– not an API code, it may change in each build.</li></ul><p>Java annotations are used to distinguish which API scope in the provided code belongs to. Annotations of the type (class, interface, enum) <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8196ea98-5b2f-4090-80cf-de4c0fb03f04">declaration</ac:inline-comment-marker>:</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p style="text-align: left;">Annotation</p></th><th><p style="text-align: left;">API Scope</p></th><th><p style="text-align: left;">Scope applies to</p></th></tr><tr><td><p style="text-align: left;">@OpenApiAll</p></td><td><p>OpenAPI</p></td><td><p>The annotated type declaration and all declared members, constructors.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@OpenApi</p></td><td><p>OpenAPI</p></td><td><p>The annotated type declaration only. Each declared member, constructor is annotated explicitly with the @OpenApi or @InternalApi annotation (see the following table).</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@InternalApi</p></td><td><p>InternalAPI</p></td><td><p>The annotated type declaration and all declared members, constructors.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@NotApi</p></td><td><p>NotAPI</p></td><td><p>The annotated type declaration and all declared members, constructors.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p>Annotations of the member, constructor declaration of the @OpenApi type:</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><p style="text-align: left;">Annotation</p></th><th><p style="text-align: left;">API Scope</p></th><th><p style="text-align: left;">Scope applies to</p></th></tr><tr><td><p>@OpenApi</p></td><td><p>OpenAPI</p></td><td><p>The annotated declared member or constructor.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>@InternalApi</p></td><td><p>InternalAPI</p></td><td><p>The annotated declared member or constructor.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p>Only code from the OpenAPI scope should be used to extend the modeling tool. All the InternalAPI and NotAPI scope code are additionally annotated with the <em>@Deprecated</em> annotation. This allows seeing the illegal API usage in all modern IDEs without an additional configuration.</p><p>We provide a set of plugin samples in <em><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="eeeaea8f-b523-4d12-a6f1-a68f5661d9d1">&lt;<em>modeling tool installation directory</em>&gt;\openapi\examples</ac:inline-comment-marker></em>. By using these examples, you can find out on how to use an OpenAPI.</p><p>In the generated javadoc, you will find the detailed description of classes, their attributes, and operations. JavaDocs are located in <em>&lt;<em>modeling tool installation directory</em>&gt;\openapi\docs</em>.</p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715084 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00438: Developing plugins using IDE

- page_id: `136715084`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715084/Developing+plugins+using+IDE
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:04.502+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Plugins
- labels: ['eclpise', 'intellij']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Plugins extending your modeling tool can be effectively developed using popular IDEs (Integrated Development Environment) such as Eclipse, IntelliJ, and others. Developers may use theirs favorite IDE for an agile modeling tool plugin coding, building, testing, and debugging. This chapter describes the plugin development in Eclipse ([www.eclipse.org](http://www.eclipse.org/)) and IntelliJ IDEA ([www.jetbrains.com](http://www.jetbrains.com/)) IDEs.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Plugins extending your modeling tool can be effectively developed using popular IDEs (Integrated Development Environment) such as Eclipse, IntelliJ, and others. Developers may use theirs favorite IDE for an agile modeling tool plugin coding, building, testing, and debugging. This chapter describes the plugin development in Eclipse (<a href="http://www.eclipse.org/">www.eclipse.org</a>) and IntelliJ IDEA (<a href="http://www.jetbrains.com/">www.jetbrains.com</a>) IDEs.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="91d26bab-dc7b-4c36-9466-26315846dc64"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="92ac631c-7363-4f6c-baa3-80257d3a1295"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715085 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00439: Development in Eclipse

- page_id: `136715085`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715085/Development+in+Eclipse
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-09-23T08:07:50.832+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Plugins > Developing plugins using IDE
- labels: ['setup-eclipse-environment']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The preconfigured Eclipse projects for two sample plugins and a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Running programs in batch mode' space=''] (command-line) tool are provided with a program installation. These projects can be found in *<modeling tool installation directory>/openapi/ide/eclipse.zip*.

Let's use MagicDraw as an example to describe the following procedures.

To setup the Eclipse environment for the modeling tool development

1. Import java projects from <*modeling tool installation directory>/openapi/ide/eclipse.zip*into your Eclipse Workspace: 
1.1. In the main menu, click **File** > **Import**. The **Import** dialog opens. 
1.2. In the **Select an import wizard** list, select**General**> **Existing Projects into Workspace**and click**Next**. 
1.3. Copy the java projects zip location to the **Select archive file** box or click **Browse** and browse to the zip file. The project list appears in the **Projects** box. 
1.4. Select projects you need and click **Finish.** [ATTACHMENT filename='import_projects.png']
2. Open the **Package Explorer** view, expand the MagicDraw project and edit the MAGIC_DRAW_INSTALL_DIRECTORY link: 
2.1. Right-click the link and select**Properties**. 
2.2. On the left side of the opened dialog, select **Resource**and click the **Edit**button on the right side of the dialog. The **Edit Link Location**dialog opens. 
2.3. Select the location and click **OK**. Be sure the link points to your MagicDraw (or other according to a modeling tool you are using) installation directory (see the following figure). [IMAGE alt='' src='']
3. Make sure that precisely Java 17 version is used by the **MagicDraw** project in your Eclipse IDE: 
3.1. Right-click the **MagicDraw** project and select**Properties**. 
3.2. On the left side of the open dialog, select **Java Build Path** and then select **Libraries** tab on the right. 
3.3. Double-click **JRE System Library [JavaSE-17]**. 
3.4. Click **Installed JREs** and check if Java 17 is available here. If there is no Java 17, add it. *Java 17 version must be added, because later versions may not be supported by our tool.* 3.5. Click **Apply and Close**. 3.6. Select **Execution environment** check box and in the combo box, select **JavaSE-17**. 3.7. C lick **Environments** button. 
3.8. In the **Execution Environments** list, select **JavaSE-17** and in the **Compatible JREs** list, select Java 17. 
3.9.Click **Apply and Close** and then **Finish** buttons. 
3.10.Select **Order and Export** tab, click **Select All** button to select all check boxes. 
3.11. Click**Apply and Close**.

Eclipse Workspace is ready for the source code development and running/debugging.

To use one of the prepared launch configurations

- On the main menu, click Run > Run Configurations... (or Debug Configurations... ). The Run Configurations dialog opens.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The launch configuration is designed to load plugins from the MagicDraw (or other according to a modeling tool you are using ) installation directory () and two plugins from the Eclipse Workspace. Thus, if the *md.plugins.dir* java system property is not defined (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Plugins directories' space='']), developing plugins are not loaded.

- MagicDraw with all plugins launches MagicDraw (or other modeling tool) with plugins available in the IDE workspace namely My Plugin 1 and My Plugin 2 .
- My Commandline launches batch mode tool My Commandline .
- MyPlugin1JUnit<n>Test launches sample JUnit<n> test.

The libraries (jar files) of the plugin must be added to the development class path throughout the plugin dependency hierarchy if the developing code depends on that plugin. 
For example, if the code depends on plugin A; plugin A depends on plugins B and C; plugin B depends on plugin D, the libraries of all plugins (A, B, C, and D) must be added to the class path. 
For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Identifying plugin dependencies' space=''] page.

When you launch your own plugin, you need to add all jar filesthat are required by your pluginfrom appropriate plugins. The MagicDraw jar files can be found in *MAGIC_DRAW_INSTALL_DIRECTORY/lib* and its sub directories, whereas plugins' jar files can be found in *MAGIC_DRAW_INSTALL_DIRECTORY/plugins* and its sub directories.

Even if the plugin descriptor file contains information about the runtime plugin *.jar* file, it is not necessary to build and deploy this *.jar* file to a plugin directory while the plugin is developed under Eclipse.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

These projects can be found in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;/openapi/ide/eclipse.zip</em>.</p><p>Let's use MagicDraw as an example to describe the following procedures.</p><p><br /></p><p>To setup the Eclipse environment for the modeling tool development</p><hr /><ol><li><p>Import java projects from &lt;<em style="line-height: 1.42857;">modeling tool installation directory&gt;/openapi/ide/eclipse.zip </em>into your Eclipse Workspace:<br />1.1.
